coredata.dc:title: Insight into the role of graphene quantum dots on the boosted photocatalytic H2 production performance of a covalent organic framework
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunxia
Zheng, Fuhang
Zhang, Lihua
Yang, Jiacheng
Dong, Pengyu
coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic activity of covalent organic frameworks (COFs) to evolve hydrogen (H2) triggered by visible light has been promising, but the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs still have to be improved. In the study, we designed and synthesized a kind of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified β-ketoenamine-linked COF composite (GQDs/COF) by an in-situ method to improve its photocatalytic H2 evolution for the first time. The resulting 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF shows a rate of H2 production of 487 μmol g−1h−1 with the illumination of visible light, outperforming those of bare TpPa-1-COF by 2.9 times. It is found that the GQDs serve as a photosensitizer that effectively enhances the optical absorption ability. Moreover, GQDs serve as a means of collecting photoinduced electrons as for preventing the subsequent recombination of carriers excited by visible light. Furthermore, the Ohmic contact effect between the TpPa-1-COF and GQDs facilitates the highly efficient migration of photoinduced electrons, which has been demonstrated by the density functional theory (DFT) calculation results. Overall, this work provides a novel strategy to design COF-based composites and gives some insight into the role of GQDs on enhanced photocatalytic activity.

head.ce:title: Insight into the role of graphene quantum dots on the boosted photocatalytic H2 production performance of a covalent organic framework

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic activity of covalent organic frameworks (COFs) to evolve hydrogen (H2) triggered by visible light has been promising, but the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs still have to be improved. In the study, we designed and synthesized a kind of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified β-ketoenamine-linked COF composite (GQDs/COF) by an in-situ method to improve its photocatalytic H2 evolution for the first time. The resulting 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF shows a rate of H2 production of 487 μmol g−1h−1 with the illumination of visible light, outperforming those of bare TpPa-1-COF by 2.9 times. It is found that the GQDs serve as a photosensitizer that effectively enhances the optical absorption ability. Moreover, GQDs serve as a means of collecting photoinduced electrons as for preventing the subsequent recombination of carriers excited by visible light. Furthermore, the Ohmic contact effect between the TpPa-1-COF and GQDs facilitates the highly efficient migration of photoinduced electrons, which has been demonstrated by the density functional theory (DFT) calculation results. Overall, this work provides a novel strategy to design COF-based composites and gives some insight into the role of GQDs on enhanced photocatalytic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the fast-rising global energy demand, it is an important mission to develop and fully utilize renewable energy sources and to assist in the transition of varied energy systems. With the help of adequate photocatalysts or photoelectrodes and plentiful water sources, hydrogen (H2) is regarded as a clean fuel that could be generated [1,2]. However, the creation of significant amounts of H2 often involves the labor-intensive, pricey, and ecologically harmful process of reforming coal, oil, and natural gas. Consequently, many projects have been completed to find practical, affordable, and environmentally responsible ways to manufacture H2 [3,4]. It is crucial to find novel photocatalysts and use them to split water to produce H2 based on improved visible light harvesting and photocatalytic activity [5,6].

Covalent organic frameworks (COFs), which exhibit wide visible-light absorption, adjustable band gaps, periodic organic elements, and high porosity, have gained recognition as an intriguing new kind of organic porous material [7–10]. In the most recent years, a broad variety of techniques have been presented in the available literature, demonstrating effective ways to raise the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of COFs [11–14]. For instance, Zhang's group conducted a thorough investigation on the creation of a heterojunction by logically combining the distinct properties of both inorganic TiO2 and organic COF semiconductors by covalent bonds, which is an extremely successful technique for strengthening the photocatalytic activity of materials [15]. Besides, the group developed a brand-new class of Z-scheme hybrids according to α-Fe2O3/TpPa-2-COF [16]. Additionally, a variety of composite materials were created and manufactured, including Ni(OH)2-X%/TpPa-2 (where X represents the molar fraction of Ni(OH)2) [17], MOF/COF hybrid materials [18], and MoS2/COF [19]. Our earlier research has proven that a two-dimensional (2D) β-ketoenamine-linked COF (TpPa-1-COF) with extraordinary chemical stability was utilized in order to embed [Mo3S13]2− nanoclusters or anchor Pt single atoms in its micropores in order to enhance the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [20,21]. In addition, we established a brand-new heterojunction photocatalyst with a 2D/2D conjugated S-scheme made up of g-C3N4 nanosheets and TpPa-1-COF [22]. Moreover, to enhance photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, we also successfully produced a hybrid material of TpPa-1-COF/ZnIn2S4 S-scheme heterojunction [23].

For simplifying photocarrier separation in photocatalytic HER, quantum dots (QDs) have been utilized as cocatalysts for reduction or oxidation, respectively, which collect and receive photogenerated holes (h+) or electrons (e−) [24]. For instance, to encourage charge separation in photocatalysis, high conductivity carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) commonly function as mediators for charge storage and electron acceptors [25,26]. GQDs are a subclass of carbon-based materials with dimensions under 20 nm and are made up of one or more graphene layers [27], which showed significant quantum confinement effects [28], consistent photoluminescence (PL) [29], limited toxicity [30], and chemical stability [31]. For usage in applications related to biology, optoelectronics, environment, and energy, GQDs have been referred to as revolutionary semiconductors. Recent research has opened up a new avenue for using GQDs as an electron or hole intermediary to enable effective photocarrier transmission. Moreover, compared with the traditional co-catalyst (e.g., Pt nanoparticles) for photocatalytic H2 evolution, GQDs exhibit a huge cost advantage and the richness of the earth's resources. For instance, Shi's research group discovered N-doped GQDs/ZnNb2O6/g-C3N4 heterostructures, in which the enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution was ascribed to the generation of more electron-holes pairs and the acceleration of the interfacial charge separation [32]. Yan et al. found that the narrow bandgap of GQDs might increase light absorption fraction and range and GQDs have a higher percentage of active sites (mainly edges) compared with graphene oxide sheets [33]. Lei et al. discovered that in a densely linked CdS/GQDs hybrid, the GQDs primarily served as a potent electron acceptor rather than a photosensitizer [25]. The explanation was the reality that the H2 evolution speed for CdS/GQDs varied from the absorption spectrum in order at longer wavelengths, but H2 creation could not be attributed to the increased light absorption of CdS/GQDs. In contrast, Wang et al. modified GQDs on tubular phosphorus-doped g-C3N4 (P-TCN) using the freeze-drying method to create a composite photocatalyst made of GQDs and PTCN, in which the GQDs showed the dual ability to serve as a photosensitizer and an electron acceptor, increasing visible light that could be absorbed and improving the separation of photoinduced e−–h+ couples in photocatalytic process [34]. Hence, it is controversial that GQDs perform a variety of functions in various photocatalytic systems.

In this work, β-ketoenamine-linked TpPa-1-COF with two-dimensional structural characteristics regulated by GQDs was created and prepared properly through synchronous integration, named GQDs/TpPa-1-COF. Systematic analysis of the morphologies, structures, and characteristics of GQDs/TpPa-1-COF photocatalysts was carried out. Finally, we thoroughly investigated the mechanism of charge immigration based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

P-toluene sulphonic acid (PTSA, AR, 99%) from Shandong West Asia Chemical Industry Company Limited was acquired; 1, 3, 5-triformylphloroglucinol (Tp, AR, 97%) was acquired from Zhengzhou Alpha Chemical Company Limited; p-phenylenediamine (Pa-1, AR, 99.5%), acetone (AR, 99.5%) and N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC, AR, 99%) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company Limited. GQDs solution (1 mg mL−1) was purchased from Suzhou Carbon Feng Graphene Technology Company Limited without additional purification.

To prepare GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, the technique outlined in the literature for the synthesis of TpPa-1-COF was referred to [35], except using the GQDs solution to replace the water. The details for preparing GQDs/TpPa-1-COF can be described as follows. P-toluene sulphonic acid (PTSA) (434.8 mg, 2.5 mmol) and p-phenylenediamine (Pa-1) (48.6 mg, 0.45 mmol) were added to the mortar and ground for 5 min. Then, 1, 3, 5-triformylphloroglucinol (Tp) (64.9 mg, 0.3 mmol) was added and milled for 10 min until a specific hue change was observed. Then, dropwise additions of varied amounts of GQDs solution (1 mg mL−1) were made to the mixture, and it was then processed once more for 5 min until there was a certain color change. It is essential to be aware that the surrounding temperature is ca. 22 °C and the relative humidity is ca. 60%, which was performed in a constant temperature and humidity chamber. And then the mixture was heated for 120 s in an oven that had been heated to 170 °C. The response mixture was chilled until it reached room temperature and poured into deionized water. The insufficiently dissolved crude sample was washed with DMAC, deionized water, and acetone, respectively, in turn a few times. The 12 h of vacuum-sealed drying at 60 °C resulted in the creation of the dark red powder by grinding. It is important to note that by adjusting the extra amount of GQDs, distinct GQDs/TpPa-1 with varying GQDs loading quantities could be achieved, which were 100, 150, 200, and 250 μL for GQDs/TpPa-1 with nominal GQDs loading quantities of 0.22, 0.33, 0.44, and 0.55 wt%, respectively.

Using a powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Panalytical X'Pert3 Powder), the crystal structures were determined. On a NICOLET NEXUS-670, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected. The samples were degassed for 12 h at 150 °C in a vacuum before analysis, and then the permanent porosities were determined employing a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 analyzer to continuously monitor the N2 adsorption-desorption tests at 77 K. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Nova Nano SEM 450) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, 200 kV) were used to examine the morphology, and measurements were made utilizing a JEM-ARM300F2 microscope (JEOL, Japan) running at 300 kV. An analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) was carried out in an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi) to investigate the samples. Utilizing 380 nm as the excitation source, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980, Edinburgh, UK). The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), photocurrent-time (I–t) response, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves, and Mott–Schottky plots have been performed utilizing the CHI660E electrochemical working station. A typical three-electrode system was utilized, where the photocatalyst coating on indium tin oxide (ITO) served as the electrode of working, a proprietary Pt plate was used as the counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrode functioned as the reference electrode, and the concomitant 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution was employed as the electrolyte. Using BaSO4 as a reference material on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer, diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) in the UV–vis range were recorded.

The as-synthesized sample (20 mg) was suspended in a 100 mL solution of 0.1 mol L−1 ascorbic acid (AC) in the photocatalytic reactor. The purpose of this step was to provide the basic conditions for the experimental setup, guaranteeing that the as-synthesized sample was present in the reaction media, and facilitate future analyses. After that, the resultant dispersion was put into a Pyrex reactor that was equipped with a shut-down gas system and a top-irradiation setup. The reactor was then exposed to vacuum pumping for around 30 min after that. In order to emit visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm), a 300 W Xe lamp (CEL-HXF300) equipped with a 420 nm cut-off filter at a 265 mW cm−2 light intensity was used as the light source. Throughout the process, cooling water was recirculated through the reaction vessel to allow this solution's temperature at 15 °C. Meanwhile, the carrier gas utilized was high-purity Ar, and gas chromatography (GC-2011, TCD) was performed to measure the evolved H2 gas. This extensive experimental setup made it possible to precisely track and examine the H2 generation process. Additionally, in equal process conditions, the photocatalytic stability was tested for 5 cycles (30 h). A similar experimental setup was also used to test the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of the evolution of H2 in addition to the illumination source. The controlled irradiation of monochromatic light was accomplished using a variety of bandpass filters installed in this Xe lamp at wavelengths of 420, 450, 500, 550, and 600 nm. By precisely measuring the AQE, this arrangement was able to shed light on the effectiveness of H2 evolution over a range of particular wavelengths [19]. The optical power metering Newport 843-R was utilized to precisely measure the intensity of photons at every distinct wavelength and quantify the monochromatic photon fluxes at different wavelengths. Thus, AQE could be calculated using Eq. (1).

Utilizing periodic boundary conditions, DFT computations have been carried out via the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) [36]. The interaction between ions and electrons was employed to explain the projector augmented wave (PAW) approach. The Perdew–Burke–Eznerhof (PBE) functionality and the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) were utilized to model electron exchange and association. After assessing more significant information to determine the correctness of the calculations, 400 eV cut-off energy was chosen. Besides that, the Monkhorst-Pack sampling strategy was utilized to sample the Brillouin zone, adopting a k-point grid. To avoid periodic unit cell conflict, a 20 Å vacuum slab was used. Furthermore, the energy adjustment of the inter-plate dipole interaction in the direction normal to the surface (IDIPOL = 3) was taken into account. The intention of employing grime's DFT-D3 technique with Becke-Jonson damping was adopted for a more accurate representation of van der Waals interactions [37]. To ensure accurate and reliable simulations, strict convergence criteria were implemented during the computational calculations. Specifically, the energy difference's convergence requirements were decided at 0.005 eV Å−1 and 1.0 × 10−6 eV.

At the beginning of preparing TpPa-1-COF using a molecular organization strategy [35], PTSA and Pa-1 were mixed and ground, after which Tp in the ingredients was added, with the subsequent addition of ∼100 μL of water. It is noted that the purpose of the added water in the mixture of PTSA, Pa-1, and Tp is to prevent COF from forming in the forward reaction and to sustain bond formation and breakdown. Thus, this systematically promotes crystallinity in a highly ordered molecular framework [38,39]. Compared with the synthesis process of TpPa-1-COF, the GQDs solution was added to the paste containing the raw materials of PTSA, Pa-1, and Tp to replace the water to prepare the GQDs/TpPa-1-COF sample. Thereby, the GQDs were deposited onto the surface of the precursor of TpPa-1-COF by this in-situ method. The synthesis of GQDs/TpPa-1-COF was accomplished successfully when it was further heated at 170 °C for 2 min. The associated multistep synthetic procedure for creating GQDs/TpPa-1-COF photocatalyst is shown in Scheme 1 .

The phase composition and structure of the crystals of the produced samples were meticulously investigated using XRD patterns. As demonstrated by Fig. 1 a, the XRD patterns exhibit three distinct and prominent peaks at approximately 4.8, 8.4, and ∼27.4°, which can be directly correlated to the reflections originating from the crystallographic planes (100), (200), and (001), respectively. These distinct peaks align closely with those described in earlier literature [40]. The relatively broad peak observed at a higher 2θ value of approximately 27.4° primarily arises from the significant π–π stacking actions occurring between the different layers of the TpPa-1-COF. Moreover, the significantly extreme sharpness of these XRD peaks is a clear indication of the exceptional crystalline quality attained in the synthesized TpPa-1-COF sample. Furthermore, none of the XRD peaks associated with crystalline GQDs are detected in any of the GQDs/TpPa-1-COF samples, indicating the effective dispersion of the GQDs within the TpPa-1-COF surface.

The identification of functional organic units that comprised the samples was accomplished through the implementation of FT-IR spectroscopy. As apparent from Fig. 1b, it is clearly evident that four distinct bands centered at approximately ∼3433, ∼1620, 1451, and 1252 cm−1 are displayed in TpPa-1-COF and GQDs/TpPa-1-COF. The broad peak observed around ∼3433 cm−1 can be linked to the stretching vibration of N–H bonds [41]. The CO stretching band, which is located at 1620 cm−1, is due to the powerful vibration caused by stretching peaks at 1580–1620 cm−1, accompanied by a shoulder representing the merged CC stretching band at approximately 1580 cm−1 [42]. At 1451 and 1252 cm−1, respectively, the stretching vibrations of CC and C–N bonds that are a part of the structure of the TpPa-1-COF are discovered [35]. The characteristic vibrational peaks of GQDs do not appear clearly in the FT-IR spectra, and these results indicate that the fundamental structure of GQDs/TpPa-1-COF remains largely unaffected. Observationally, it can be deduced that the addition of GQDs does not cause substantial disruption to the underlying framework of TpPa-1-COF.

The permanent porosities of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF were determined by employing N2 sorption examination at 77 K. Both TpPa-1-COF and GQDs/TpPa-1-COF samples display a characteristic type I isotherm, as clearly indicated by the adsorption curves in Fig. 1c. This isotherm is commonly associated with microporous materials, suggesting the presence of well-defined and interconnected micropores within the structures of these samples. The highest pore size of the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF is around 1.18 nm, while the maximum pore size of TpPa-1-COF is around 1.26 nm (Fig. 1d). This observation proposes a significant crystallinity and structural order within the materials. Table S1 provides an overview of the various surface areas identified using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) approach, which are 211 and 203 m2 g−1 for the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, individually. The 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF exhibits a slightly inferior surface area than the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, probably due to the blocking of GQDs onto the micropore of TpPa-1-COF.

SEM and TEM were utilized to analyze the morphological characteristics of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF. The SEM images of the unadulterated sample show clear fibrous morphology (Fig. 2 a, b), while the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF display a clear layered structure (Fig. 2c, d). Fig. 2e, f demonstrates that the particle size of GQDs nanoparticles is around 5–10 nm. TEM and HRTEM images of bare TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF are compared. It is observed that there are no nanoparticles and lattice fringes in the sample of bare TpPa-1-COF (Fig. 2g–i) while both nanoparticles ascribed to GQDs and the lattice spacing of 0.32 nm attributed to the (002) plane of GQDs are presented in the sample of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF (Fig. 2j–l), demonstrating that GQDs are distributed on the surface of TpPa-1-COF.

By applying the entire survey XPS spectra, it is possible to determine the surface chemical components of the TpPa-1-COF as well as the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF crystals. The survey XPS peaks of these two samples clearly demonstrate the existence of the elements of C, N, and O (Fig. 3 a). According to Fig. 3b, upon careful observation of the C 1s spectrum, it is evident that the peaks observed at 288.1, 286.3, and 284.6 eV are confidently ascribed to specific carbon species in the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF. The peak at 288.1 eV corresponds to the existence of CO bonds; the peak at 286.3 eV is attributed to C–N bonds; and the peak at 284.6 eV is ascribed to C–C/CC bonds. It is noteworthy that the observed peak positions and assignments in the C 1s spectra of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF can be matched to the reported β-ketoenamine-based COFs [21]. Fig. 3c shows that the N 1s spectra have a prominent peak at 400.2 eV, and this peak is correlated with the C–NHx groups [43]. In comparison to the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, the C–NHx groups of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF exhibit a mild shift toward the higher-energy side, suggesting that N atoms have a lower electron density. As displayed in Fig. 3d, it can be observed that two distinct peaks appear at 531.0 and 533.0 eV. The peak at 531.0 eV corresponds to the existence of CO bonds, and the peak at 533.0 eV can be attributed to the presence of end aldehyde groups (–CHO) in the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF [23]. Notably, the –CHO and CO groups of the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF sample reveal a little movement in the direction of higher energy compared to the TpPa-1-COF. It could be a result of the transport of electrons from TpPa-1-COF onto GQDs of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, and it is similar to the electron transfer in the Pt1@TpPa-1-COF system [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2 evolution activity and stability
ce:section.ce:para:
The standard calibration curve of H2 detection is shown in Fig. S1. The generated amount of H2 evolved was analyzed by chromatography with a known concentration of H2 as standard gas. According to the fitted standard curve, the relationship between the peak area and the volume of H2 evolved can be expressed as Eq. (2).
(2)
y = 116833 x + 1707
where y is the peak area corresponding to H2 in the chromatography, and x is the volume of H2 evolved (mL).

The volume of H2 produced by the photocatalytic water splitting could be calculated by replacing the peak area measured by the experiment of photocatalytic H2 evolution with the standard curve. An example of the H2 evolved chromatogram is illustrated in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3, which shows the evolution process of the peak areas over TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF photocatalysts with different irradiation times. The amount of H2 evolved was measured every 1 h using an online gas chromatograph instrument with a thermal conductivity detector. The measured peak area was transformed into the H2 evolution rate based on the standard curve (Eq. (2)).

The number of generated moles (μmol) of H2 generated can be expressed as Eq. (3).
(3)
n =
V
23.7
where n (μmol) is the number of moles of H2 produced, V (mL) is the hydrogen volume evolved, and 23.7 (mL mmol−1) is the molar volume of the gas due to the temperature of the gas phase being 15 °C.

Moreover, the number of moles (μmol g−1) of hydrogen produced per mass of photocatalyst can be expressed as Eq. (4).

where C(H2) (μmol g−1) is the number of moles of hydrogen produced per mass of photocatalyst and m (g) is the amount of photocatalyst added in the photocatalytic reactor.

where P is the amount of H2 produced per gram of photocatalyst per hour (μmol g−1h−1), and
P

is the mean value of P (μmol g−1h−1).

Triggered by visible light, the photocatalytic performance for H2 evolution of the as-produced materials was investigated. As described in Fig. 4 , photocatalytic H2 evolution exposed to visible light irradiation is used to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of all the as-synthesized samples. The time-dependent photocatalytic H2 evolution per mass of all samples is displayed in Fig. 4a. It is evident that the levels of H2 evolution significantly rise when illumination time is increased. Compared to the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF sample shows unquestionably better photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution. Fig. 4b illustrates a succession of x% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF photocatalysts featuring H2 evolution rates of 167, 189, 407, 487, and 122 μmol g−1h−1, for × = 0, 0.22, 0.33, 0.44, and 0.55, respectively. The most notable photocatalytic H2 evolution rate is demonstrated by the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF photocatalyst, which is 2.9 times stronger than that presented by the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF. The high H2 evolution rate of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF is considerable compared with the previous works on COF-based photocatalysts, as listed in Table S2. Additionally, it is noted that excessive GQDs loading (such as 0.55% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF) can impair photocatalytic effectiveness, given that high loading of GQDs might affect the optical absorption and the water molecule adsorption properties of the catalyst. When the surface of TpPa-1-COF becomes stronger covered by GQDs, less light reaches the TpPa-1-COF material to produce electron-hole-pairs, which is adverse to the photocatalytic H2 evolution. As presented in Fig. 4c, the current AQE trend coincides with that of the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, further demonstrating that this material's ability to absorb light encourages the creation of H2 [44]. It demonstrates that the AQE value (0.65% at 420 nm) of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF catalyst outperform the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF in regard to photocatalytic activity. It is also noteworthy that 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF can exceptionally well preserve its photocatalytic H2 evolution capability at its preliminary level after an additional 5 cycles (Fig. 4d). The photocatalytic activity of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF has a high degree of repeatability, which points to outstanding stability over 30 h.

PL spectroscopy could be utilized to investigate the photoelectron-hole recombination mechanism. It is well known that radiation-induced recombination and nonradiative dampening activities are related to the efficiency and intensity of PL emission in line with Eq. (7) [45].
(7)
η =
I r
/
(
I r
+
I
nr
)
where η represents the PL emission efficiency, Ir is the probability of a radiative transition, and Inr is the likelihood of a nonradiative transition, respectively.

The charge carrier's radiative recombination was thought to be the root cause of the improved PL emission efficiency, in contrast to its nonradiative recombination, which was thought to be the explanation of the decreased PL emission efficiency [46]. The pace between the photogenerated charges recombine and the PL intensity is always negatively correlated. According to Fig. 5 a, the PL spectrum of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF exhibits a prominent emission peak at 574 nm as a consequence of the recombination of photogenerated carriers from band-to-band [47]. Additionally, the PL intensity of the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF sample is considerably below that corresponding to the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, establishing that photogenerated carrier recombination is extremely hindered in the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF sample. As demonstrated in Fig. 5b, in the on–off cycle mode and under conditions of visible light, the photocurrent density was calculated. It was discovered that the photocurrent intensities of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF sample and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF are 0.31 and 0.63 μA cm−2, respectively. Without a doubt, this sample containing 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF has the highest photocurrent responsiveness, in conformity with the effectiveness of photocatalytic H2 production. The photoelectrochemical approach was used to further explore the charge-transfer kinetics. The diameter of the semicircle on the EIS Nyquist plot is equivalent to the carriers' resistance to charges being transported via them. As depicted in Fig. 5c, it demonstrates that the EIS Nyquist semicircle of the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF has a far smaller diameter as compared to the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. 5c, the EIS data are fitted using the Z-view application using the analogous circuitry model. The charge-transfer resistance is denoted by Rct while the series resistance is denoted by Rs [48]. According to the fitting findings, the Rct of the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF sample is a much smaller diameter as compared to the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, indicating that the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF sample has higher charge migration capability and less interfacial charge transfer resistance. In addition, the LSV curves for the photoanodes are depicted in Fig. 5d, manifesting that the current density of the photocatalysts increases in tandem with the bias voltage. Distinctively, the initial potentials of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF are correspondingly 0.44 and 0.25 V (vs. NHE). Notably, when the current density is identical in comparison to the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF composite has a substantially lower initial potential for H2 evolution, demonstrating that GQDs improved the H2 generation kinetics of GQDs/TpPa-1-COF composites. The LSV curves of the other samples (e.g., 0.22% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, 0.33% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, and 0.55% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF) further confirm this consequence (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical absorption property and band-level position
ce:section.ce:para:
In this current investigation, the light-harvesting ability of the synthetic samples was assessed utilizing UV–vis DRS. According to Fig. 6 a, the GQDs/TpPa-1-COF composites exhibit an improved visible light intensity of absorption throughout the 400–700 nm range compared with the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, demonstrating that GQDs/TpPa-1-COF composites are more effective visible-light-driven photocatalysts that possess a broad light absorption range. It could be because the GQDs act as photosensitizers to improve visible light absorption. Additionally, the energy of bandgap (Eg) is estimated utilizing Tauc’s Eq. (8) [49].
(8)
α h v =
A ( h v -
E g
)
n
where α indicates the absorption coefficient, A indicates the absorption constant, and n is a constant that relies on the existence of transitions; for the permitted direct and indirect transitions, it uses values of 2 and 1/2, respectively.

Hence, it is possible to determine the bandgap energy (Eg) in the samples by examining the tangent intercept of the (αhv)2 vs. photon energy (hv) plots given that TpPa-1-COF exhibits direct bandgap characteristics as a semiconductor [18,50]. Significantly, the determination of the optical bandgap relies on the analysis of Tauc plots, as illustrated in Fig. 6b and Fig. S5. We observed that the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF composite exhibits a comparatively narrower bandgap of 2.02 eV in contrast to the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, which possesses a bandgap of 2.05 eV. This variation suggests that the incorporation of GQDs in the TpPa-1-COF structures has a slight effect on the electronic properties, specifically the bandgap.

Furthermore, Mott–Schottky plots were generated to provide deeper insights into the energy band positions of both the as-synthesized unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and GQDs/TpPa-1-COF composites. Mott–Schottky plots at different frequencies (500 Hz, 1000 Hz, and 1500 Hz) were measured. As depicted in Fig. 6c, d, the typical n-type semiconductor of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF can be seen in the positively sloping linear area. Moreover, the flat band potential (Efb) for the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, which is –0.49 and –0.68 V vs. NHE when pH = 0, is also reflected in the x-intercept. It is also found that the Efb of these two samples is frequency independent. The slope of the Mott-Schottky-plot is always associated with the charge carrier density and a decreased slope of the Mott-Schottky-plot means a higher charge carrier density [51]. In our case, the slope of the Mott-Schottky-plot of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1 at higher potential (e.g. above 0.5 V) measured at 1000 Hz is smaller than that of TpPa-1 (Fig. 6e), indicating that 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1 exhibits a higher charge carrier density, which is beneficial to the photocatalytic H2 evolution. In addition, Mott–Schottky plots of the other samples (e.g., 0.22% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, 0.33% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, and 0.55% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF) are displayed in Fig. S6. According to Fig. 6f, the ECBM position is very close to the Efb of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF. And thus, the positions of the energy band levels of both the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and the 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF are summarized. As shown in Fig. S7, the ECBM and EVBM values are estimated to be –0.43 and 1.60 V of 0.22% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, –0.52 and 1.41 V of 0.33% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, and –0.74 and 1.24 V of 0.55% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, respectively. The EVBM and ECBM values align throughout in terms of the kinetic energy levels associated with the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the organic photocatalysts, respectively. The electrochemical energy band location of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and GQDs/TpPa-1-COF is determined to assess their potential for the photolysis of water and hydrogen evolution. It is observed that the potentials of ECBM for both materials are more negative compared to the standard hydrogen electrode (H+/H2) reference potential (0 V vs. NHE at pH = 0). In this case, it appears that GQDs/TpPa-1-COF is a potential photocatalyst, allowing water splitting during photolysis to produce H2.

To demonstrate the charge transfer in the GQDs/TpPa-1-COF system, DFT calculations were employed. Fig. 7 a, b displays the surfaces of TpPa-1-COF (001) and GQDs (001) with their respective electrostatic potentials. As a result, the work functions (Φ) for the optimal structures for the surfaces of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF and GQDs can be calculated to be 4.75 and 4.01 eV, respectively. It appears that TpPa-1-COF displays a greater Fermi level than GQDs because the Φ of TpPa-1-COF is bigger than that of GQDs. The charge moves at the interface as a result of the deviations in the Φ of TpPa-1-COF and GQDs, which causes the band edge to bend due to changes in the number of electrons until the Fermi level equilibrium is attained [52]. That's to say, the difference in Φ between GQDs and TpPa-1-COF creates a Fermi level offset, facilitating rapid charge redistribution at the interfaces of GQDs /TpPa-1-COF.

The band structures and the partial density of states (PDOS) of TpPa-1-COF and GQDs were calculated. As can be evidenced in Fig. 7c, it was observed that the roof of the HOMO and the bottom of the LUMO are both extremely plain. The lowest energy of the LUMO and the highest energy of the HOMO are localized at the same point of G, indicating the TpPa-1-COF is a semiconductor with a direct bandgap. The calculated bandgap is 1.533 eV, which is considerably lower than the test band gap of 2.05 eV. The reason is that the underestimation of the band gap in the DFT calculation is caused by the inability to accurately predict the excited state properties [53]. In addition, the calculation of the PDOS was used to evaluate the electrical band structure of TpPa-1-COF. The uppermost part of the HOMO of TpPa-1-COF is predominantly given by the 2p orbitals of the C, O, and N atoms, although the bottom portion of the LUMO is mostly given by the 2p orbitals of C atoms, according to an analysis of the PDOS at the lower end of the LUMO and the uppermost part of the HOMO, as shown in Fig. 7d. Furthermore, as can be observed in Fig. 7e, the results indicate that GQDs have an absence of bandgap, thus certifying that GQDs are conductors. Fig. 7f demonstrates that the electrical band structure of GQDs is composed of the hybridized orbitals of C s and C p.

For the purpose to lessen the mismatch within the lattice between stacked layers, a model of the materials made up of TpPa-1-COF (001) and GQDs (001) surfaces was used and adjusted, as shown in Fig. 7g. To look into the charge transfer taking place on the surfaces of TpPa-1-COF (001) and GQDs (001) in the ground state, the charge density difference analysis was done on the optimal hybridization of GQDs (001)/TpPa-1-COF (001). As shown in Fig. 7h, to produce a three-dimensional (3D) charge density difference, the computed electronic charges of the TpPa-1-COF (001) and GQDs (001) were subtracted out of the hybridization of GQDs/COF. The surface of TpPa-1-COF (001) is predominantly characterized by cyan regions, while the surface of GQDs (001) exhibits primarily yellow areas with some cyan areas present. This finding suggests that the electron was rapidly transferred throughout the interface via the TpPa-1-COF (001) surface to the GQDs (001) surface. The attainable gap in work functions mentioned previously is one hypothesis for the spontaneous interface transfer of charges from TpPa-1-COF onto the surface of GQDs when subjected to the ground-state situation.

As mentioned above, the work function of the TpPa-1-COF (001) crystal plane (4.75 eV) is much larger than that of GQDs (4.01 eV). Therefore, when they make contact with one another, it proceeds according to the Ohmic contact effect's mechanism that is in analogy with the structural model between n-type semiconductor and metal [54], in which the electrons from the LUMO of the TpPa-1-COF may flow to GQDs, causing the latter to have a downward band bend until the Fermi-level equilibrium state has been reached, as shown in Scheme 2 a. Then, a probable approach for the evolution of photocatalytic H2 generation via GQDs/TpPa-1-COF is put forth and depicted in Scheme 2b. On the one hand, the GQDs acted as a photosensitizer located onto the surface of the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF, GQDs having exceptional visible light absorption capability and taking part in the light collection. On the other hand, the photoinduced electrons of TpPa-1-COF move away from the HOMO toward the LUMO in response to visible-light irradiation. For the purpose of separating the charges produced by visible light and the transmitted charged electrons with ease, GQDs in the GQDs/TpPa-1-COF are capable of functioning as an electron collector and transporter, as confirmed by I–t, EIS, and PL mentioned above. In addition, GQDs allow GQDs/TpPa-1-COF hybrid material to have a lower initial potential, as confirmed by the LSV mentioned. Furthermore, the electrons have a higher negative potential of the LUMO in the GQDs/TpPa-1-COF, indicating they have a much stronger reduction ability, which facilitates the reduction of protons (H+). Subsequently, the photoexcited electrons reduce the H+ that is adsorbed on the most active sites of GQDs to create H2, while the matching holes left onto the unadulterated TpPa-1-COF's HOMO level are trapped with the sacrificial reagent of AC. Besides, the water contact angles measurement of the samples of TpPa-1-COF and 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF were performed, as shown in Fig. S8. It is found that the sample of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF exhibits a smaller water contact angle compared with TpPa-1-COF, indicating that the GQDs/TpPa-1-COF is more hydrophilic, which is beneficial to the photocatalytic H2 evolution.

To sum up, we establish an entirely novel kind of GQDs-modified β-ketoenamine-linked COF composite (GQDs/TpPa-1-COF) by replacing the addition of water with GQDs solution during the preparation of TpPa-1-COF. The results manifest that GQDs are deposited on the surface of TpPa-1-COF by this in-situ method. Moreover, it indicates that the introduction of GQDs significantly boosts the photocatalytic H2 production activity of TpPa-1-COF, and the best photocatalytic H2 evolution property is at an ideal GQDs/TpPa-1-COF ratio of 0.44% (487 μmol g−1h−1). The AQE value of the sample of 0.44% GQDs/TpPa-1-COF reaches 0.65% at 420 nm. Furthermore, the results demonstrate that the GQDs onto GQDs/TpPa-1-COF hybrid materials function as both a photosensitizer and electronic recipient, which improve the visible light absorption and make them have a lower initial potential, aiding in the separation and transfer of charges by photons from active electrons resulting from the Ohmic contact effect. Besides, the H+ reduction could be readily carried out using the electrons gathered in GQDs, and AC is capable of managing to absorb the holes remaining in the TpPa-1-COF's HOMO level. In addition, the enhanced hydrophilicity of GQDs/TpPa-1-COF could be another factor for the boosted photocatalytic H2 production. Overall, this research may pave the path to the future development and fabrication of novel COF-based hybrids using the synchronous composite technique for effective photocatalytic processes as well.
